intro: Transitioning from JavaScript to SQL shifts a programmer's focus from imperative, step-by-step memory management to declarative, set-based database logic. Instead of writing procedural loops and filter chains to clean data collections in application memory, you instruct the database engine on *what* records to fetch or mutate directly at the persistence layer. This transition replaces short-lived runtime variables with a long-term, indexed database engine optimized for transaction isolation.

diffs.from: Dynamic types attached to runtime values; flexible objects with loosely enforced keys.
diffs.to: Static, rigidly enforced schemas defining column types (VARCHAR, INT, UUID, TIMESTAMP) at the table definition layer.

diffs.from: Imperative, procedural script block syntax structured around variables, functions, and loop conditionals.
diffs.to: Declarative query statements composed of clauses (SELECT, JOIN, WHERE, GROUP BY) processed as unified datasets.

diffs.from: Runtimes allocate structures in volatile heap memory, cleared periodically via automatic garbage collection.
diffs.to: Managed database cache pools, transaction logs, indexes, and permanent disk allocations overseen by a relational engine.

diffs.from: Event-driven asynchronous model built on single-threaded execution loops and promise-based deferrals.
diffs.to: Synchronous request processing isolated by database connection pools, executing operations via strict transaction isolation levels.

diffs.from: Generic APIs serving environment primitives, cryptographic tools, network routing, and file system I/O.
diffs.to: Specialized domain functions built exclusively for text parsing, aggregate calculations, mathematical computation, and datetime logic.

examples.title: Filtering and Joining Related Structures
examples.description: Filtering a nested array or executing client-side data combining versus relational table joining.
examples.toCode:
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE o.total > 20;

examples.title: Aggregating Totals across Categories
examples.description: Grouping an array of data objects using reducers vs standard SQL groupings.

tips.title: Think in Sets, Not Loops
tips.body: Avoid running queries inside nested application loops (the N+1 query bug). Always aim to fetch or update records using single, combined set queries.

tips.title: Enforce Constraint Safety First
tips.body: Move your application-level data validations (like length checks or uniqueness validations) directly into database schema constraints like UNIQUE or NOT NULL.

tips.title: Utilize Indexes Strategically
tips.body: Unlike flat JavaScript arrays where finding elements is an O(N) operation, SQL tables require explicit indexing on foreign keys or search targets to remain O(log N).

tips.title: Sanitize Queries to Prevent Injection
tips.body: Never construct raw SQL query blocks via template literal string concatenation. Always use prepared statements or parameterized inputs to keep database targets secure.

faqs.question: Can I write conditional conditional logic like if/else within standard SQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, SQL uses the declarative 'CASE WHEN ... THEN ... ELSE END' expression syntax to evaluate branching logic directly inside select criteria or updates.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of JS array iteration methods inside a relational database?
faqs.answer: SQL queries utilize specific clauses: 'WHERE' acts as a filter, 'SELECT' maps columns, and 'GROUP BY' aggregates records, acting over whole datasets at once.

faqs.question: How do databases handle missing values compared to JavaScript code?
faqs.answer: JavaScript handles missing properties via 'undefined' or explicit 'null'. SQL uses a single 'NULL' token which acts under three-valued logic systems.
